w:r.w:t: All individuals active with the 412 Youth Zone had the opportunity to participate in a survey
. Surveys could be completed on paper
had the option to provide their name to be entered in a raffle for
, but they could also complete the survey anonymously
) responded to the survey, for a response rate of
Most respondents were age 18 or older.
of the respondents who reported their age were between 18 and 20 years old
More females responded to the survey than males
but males are better represented among survey respondents than in prior years.
Twelve transgender and non-binary youth also responded to the survey (7
As represented in the table below, youth of all genders selected a variety of terms to describe their sexual orientation. Y
oung women were more likely than their male peers to report identifying as
Forty-two percent of respondents identified as LGBTQ in some way.
Survey Respondents by Gender and Sexual Orientation
Gay, Lesbian, or Same Gender Loving
The tables below display respondents’ racial identities and genders.

Youth Zone participants’ racial identities cannot neatly be summarized because there is a lot of diversity within their multiracial identities. Therefore, the first table shows the demographics of all youth summarized at a high level. The table
shows how many youth identify with certain ethnic and racial backgrounds. In th
table, many youth are represented more than one time.
The demographic breakdown of youth responding to the survey is very similar to 2024, with one notable exception
he number of responses from Black males doubled this year compared to last year
ne in three survey respondents were Black females.
percent of all respondents identify as Black, either as their full identity or as one part of their multiracial identity. Similarly, 2
% of youth fully or partially identify as White,
5% as Native American or Native Hawaiian, 4
% as Hispanic or Latinx, and 3% as Asian.

percent of youth selected the term “multiracial” as either the sole term or one among others that they use to describe their racial or ethnic identity. This distribution of racial identities among respondents
Youth with Full or Partial Racial Identities As…
percent of youth reported their coaches were trustworthy
values their opinions about their life
ratings this year are slightly lower than
in prior years, the difference is minimal. Youth consistently rated th
eir coaches well nine times out of ten,
a range consistent with prior surveys
No coaches had more than one respondent provide a negative review.
Percent Often or All of the Time
Values my opinions about my life
Is available to me when I need them
Makes me feel heard and understood
contact with their coach at least weekly (
being satisfied with the frequency of their communication with their coach
who reported that their communication is
monthly or less than once per month.
The majority of them report being in stable housing.
They have different coach assignments, and t
whether their current housing is safe and stable, meaning they can stay there for at least the next 90 days.
indicated they currently live in safe and stable housing, but one
Most youth who are unstably hous
reported being somewhere safe that they could not stay,
asked where they are currently sleeping
(from a list of options with the opportunity to self
A majority indicated they are currently staying
sleeping locations were homeless shelters and couch surfing.
Housing Status and Current Sleeping Arrangements
youth who indicated they have stable housing reported sleeping at a homeless shelter. Another three indicated they live in a group home.
Regardless of their current living situation,
experienced unstable housing in the prior six months,
displays the answers by age. The most common reasons youth experienced unstable housing were
because their friends or family no longer allowed them to stay with them, or they felt unsafe at home
selected at least one of those two reasons
youth with unstable housing reported losing their housing due to eviction.
Reasons for Unstable Housing in the Past 6 Months, by Age
No unstable housing in past 6 months
Family/friends no longer let me stay with them
I left where I was living because I felt unsafe
Youth could report more than one reason for experiencing unstable housing.
Youth were asked to report on whether they
. The self-reported employment and education statuses of youth are represented in the
% of all respondents reported being enrolled in school
unemployed are looking for a job
unemployed are looking for a job
youth report not completing high school or a GED program
the youth who are currently enrolled in school are in high school
When excluding the youth who report being enrolled in high school,
of youth have their high school diploma or GED.
percent are currently enrolled in higher education or have already received a college degree or certification.
Highest Level Completed for Youth Not in School
The unemployment rate for respondents not enrolled in school (5
higher than the unemployment rate of youth enrolled in school (4
, many of whom are in high school
. This survey does not capture whether the young adults who are not working or enrolled in school have other sources of income, but it does reveal that
t working are looking for employment
% of all respondents reported leaving a job in the past year.
have been at their job for six months or longer.
have been at their jobs for fewer than
Length of Employment for Youth Currently Employed
Youth who are not enrolled in school reported being at their job for less than three months at a much higher rate than youth who are in school.
As displayed in the table below, rates of full-time versus part-time employment vary by age, with older youth more likely to hold full-time positions.
respondents had trouble finding a job in the prior twelve months, they were asked to share some of the reasons they thought were contributing to the
could also add their own reasons. Reasons given by two or more youth are listed in the table below.
indicated they do not understand why their attempts to secure employment are unsuccessful
In addition to fewer youth report
these most common reasons as barriers, the number of youth reporting drug or alcohol use as a barrier dropped
to only one respondent this year.
Reasons Youth Have Trouble Finding Jobs (Reasons Given by 2 or More People)
I don’t know. I am applying and not getting called
Note: Youth could select more than one option

The third most common reason, which increased substantially from last year (13%) to a similar level seen in 2023 (24%), was physical or mental health issues. All of the youth reporting health issues as a barrier to employment were 18 or older, and youth of all gender and racial identities were represented in this group. There are no discernable patterns among this group of youth.
lost or left a job in the past year, they were asked to share the reason
ages 21 to 23 reported leaving their jobs at higher rates than
, followed by not receiving enough pay or hours
Thirty-seven percent reported they left because they found a better job
percent of youth who left a job did so because the job was seasonal or temporary.
a Job in the Past Yea
r (Reasons Given by 2 or More People)
whether they have a driver’s license and the type of
percent of all respondents indicated they have a driver’s license, and an additional
The likelihood a young person has their license increases with age
Compared to last year, the same percentage of youth report having
among the youth who’ve started the process to get their license,
more youth this year report having their license versus a learner’s permit.
Of those with a driver’s license,
access to a reliable vehicle. About one-
have their own reliable vehicle, and
another fourth share a vehicle or can borrow one when needed
Access to a Reliable Vehicle by Age
I have my own reliable vehicle
I share a reliable vehicle with someone else
I can borrow a car when I need to
I have my own vehicle, but it often breaks down or needs repairs.
I do not usually have access to a reliable vehicle.
All youth were asked about the primary way the
get to work when they are employed.
The majority rely on public transportation.
indicate they use a mixture of methods that do not include them driving themselves (public transportation, walking, rides from others, and rideshare). Only
about one in ten youth report driving themselves to work.
Primary Way Youth Get to Work
No-Car Combination: Bus, rideshare, getting rides, walking
Getting rides from someone else (coworker, family, friend, etc.)
Combination with Car: Drive myself, bus, getting rides, rideshare
† Respondents were asked to select their
method of transportation, but some youth completing the paper survey selected multiple options. They are grouped here according to whether the individual sometimes drives.
For the first time this year,
youth were asked to report on whether they are registered to vote, and if not, the reasons why. Two-thirds of all respondents indicated they are registered to vote
, with significantly more 21- to 23-year-olds being registered than youth ages 18 to 20.
The rates of registration are the same for youth identifying as male and female (69%), but only 40% of transgender and non-binary youth are registered to vote.
This is largely due to the difference in registration
The most common reason youth provided for not registering to vote is that they believe their vote won’t make a difference.
The next most common reason was that they do not understand politics.
Seven of the youth who gave a reason (15%) indicated that they did not know how to register.
Reasons Youth Report Not Registering to Vote
My vote won't make a difference
I do not know how to register
I am not old enough to vote
duals who reported being registered to vote also checked a box for a reason they were
registered. This may indicate they’re registered but unlikely to vote. Four of the
their vote won’t make a difference.
experiences at the Zone, as well as how youth perceived the services provided by Zone staff to be beneficial to
of respondents fully agreed that support from the Youth Zone helps them to stay focused on their goals.
attendance at the Zone and youth reporting that the Youth Zone helps them stay focused on
outh who report attending the Zon
e at least monthly Agree or Somewhat Agree that the Youth Zone helps them stay focused on their goals, compared to
percent of youth who report attending the Zone Never or Less than once per month
arely or never attend are also
Attendance patterns at the Zone vary by age, with
18% report attending weekly, 34% one to three times a week, and 32% less than monthly. Seventeen percent of all respondents indicated never visiting the Zone downtown.
ost youth report coming to the 412 Youth Zone downtown to see their coach and to work toward their goals.
Half of the youth who come regularly reported coming in for scheduled activities.
The other most common reasons are to learn new things
outh who come to the Zone on a
come to see their coach and
value on opportunities to have their basic needs met
than youth who come less frequently.
ng less than once per month are more likely to come to see their coach

w:r.w:t: ho never visit the Zone, or visit less than monthly, they were asked what would make them want to come more frequently. The
career and job opportunities, a library, program to get a car, and regular uncensored studio usage.
over a quarter of youth who rarely come indicated they’d be motivated by an invitation from their coach or therapist, and
knowing more about the activities would be helpful.
The potential impact of direct communication with youth about what happens at the Zone was reinforced by some
open text responses which indicated that some youth
felt anxiety about coming because they were new or weren’t familiar with the activities.
What Would Make Someone Who Rarely Visits the Zone Want to Come, by Age
they’d be motivated to come by an invitation from their therapist.

Finally, some youth indicated that there was not really anything that would make them want to come more often. Most indicated it was because they were too busy, the Zone was too far away, or they had too many parenting demands.
youth with these challenges indicated they might attend activities if there was assistance with transportation and more activities scheduled outside of the workday.
Youth Zone staff are helping young people make progress
oach or staff at the Zone have most frequently helped them
Youth Zone staff helped them to think about their future
Many of these outcomes were more frequently reported by older youth than among younger youth.
However, an increasing number of youth ages 16 to 17
are identifying assistance with housing as something they’ve received from the Zone
indicated progress supported by the Zone
Youth Zone support making a difference in two or more areas
they just started with their coach or haven’t met with them yet.
My Coach or the Youth Zone has Helped Me To… (by Age)

w:r.w:t: Respondents were asked to rate how often they felt
by both staff and their peers.
Consistent with patterns from prior years, youth reported feeling more consistently respected by staff than their peers.
they felt respected and accepted by staff
The demographics of the young people indicating they
felt treated with respect and accepted were examined
observed is that the majority (over 80%) of youth indicating they
ever felt respected and accepted by the staff or their peers were ages 21 to 23. None were
questions about their use of banks and other ways that they store, receive, and transfer money
. Banking practices vary slightly by age,
broken down by age group. Overall,
indicated they have a bank account
respondents indicated that they use a
Nearly all youth who report having an account have a checking account, and
of them also have a savings account.
he percentages of youth reporting they manage their money by using banking apps and saving money at home both increased since last year.
Sixty-five percent of all youth report using banking apps such
as their primary way of managing their money
Had an account in the past
Methods Youth Use to Store, Receive, and Transfer Money
Youth with bank accounts were asked to identify
ways they use their account. The
, starting with the most common uses
, which are saving money and using direct deposit for their sources of income.
more consistently use their bank accounts to
, and pay bills than younger youth.
16 to 17 primarily use their accounts to save money,
Ways Respondents Use Their Bank Account(s), by Age
Finally, youth had the option to share any other comments or feedback they had
took the opportunity to express their gratitude to the Youth Zone in general.
A few quotes are included below as examples of some of the responses.
Thank you for making me feel safe to open up about my situation
I enjoy coming there every time I go
The Youth Zone is a place in which I feel safe and be myself while having access to resources.
youth provided feedback on ways the Youth Zone could be improved,
should update us on certain things. I asked for them to find me a driving school still have not found one
Start making people wash when they smell bad
I feel like y’all should relocate to a
